message.abstract:
<jats:title>Summary</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="simple">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>▪</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>
                        We augment the well-known susceptible – infected – recovered – deceased (
                        <jats:italic>SIRD</jats:italic>
                        ) epidemiological model to include vaccination dynamics, implemented as a piecewise continuous simulation. We calibrate this model to reported case data in the UK at a national level,
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>▪</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>
                        Our modelling approach decouples the inherent characteristics of the infection from the degree of human interaction (as defined by the effective reproduction number,
                        <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                        <jats:sub>
                          <jats:italic>e</jats:italic>
                        </jats:sub>
                        ). This allows us to detect and infer a change in the characteristic of the infection, for example the emergence of the Kent variant,
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>▪</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>
                        We find that that the infection rate constant (
                        <jats:italic>k</jats:italic>
                        ) increases by around 89% as a result of the B.1.1.7 (Kent) COVID-19 variant in England,
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>▪</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>
                        Through retrospective analysis and modelling of early epidemic case data (between March 2020 and May 2020) we estimate that ∼1.2M COVID-19 infections were unreported in the early phase of the epidemic in the UK. We also obtain an estimate of the basic reproduction number as,
                        <jats:italic>R</jats:italic>
                        <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                        = 3.23,
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>▪</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>We use our model to assess the UK Government’s roadmap for easing the third national lockdown as a result of the current vaccination programme. To do this we use our estimated model parameters and a future forecast of the daily vaccination rates of the next few months,</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>▪</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>Our modelling predicts an increased number of daily cases as NPIs are lifted in May and June 2021,</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>▪</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>We quantify this increase in terms of the vaccine rollout rate and in particular the percentage vaccine uptake rate of eligible individuals, and show that a reduced take up of vaccination by eligible adults may lead to a significant increase in new infections.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:p>
title: Mechanistic model calibration and the dynamics of the COVID-19 epidemic in the UK (the past, the present and the future)
